food_subgroup: Rice and lentil one-pot dish with leafy greens

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general khichdi entry (FOODON_00002694); no spinach-specific khichdi variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers khichdi as a rice-lentil composite dish; no palak-specific variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for khichdi or palak khichdi. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Palak Khichdi is approximately 300 g (one bowl), as typically served in Indian restaurants and home kitchens.

nutrient_source.title: Khichdi (rice and moong dal) with spinach — composite estimate based on ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017: Rice (boiled), Moong dal (cooked), Spinach (cooked), Ghee
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry for Palak Khichdi exists. Values estimated from a weighted composite of ICMR-NIN 2017 entries: boiled rice (~60% by weight), cooked moong dal (~25%), cooked spinach (~10%), and ghee (~5%). Proportions reflect a standard home-style recipe. Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central analogous entries.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice-lentil composite dishes — analogous estimate based on Moong dal khichdi GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Palak Khichdi found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 based on moong dal khichdi (rice + moong dal composite), which has a reported GI of approximately 50 (low GI) per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. Addition of spinach (low GI vegetable) and ghee (fat slows gastric emptying) is expected to maintain or slightly lower the GI. GL per serving = (50 × 17.5 × 300/100) / 100 = 26.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g; ~630 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size, as a standard 300 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 630 mg sodium (~27% of the 2300 mg daily limit); home preparation with reduced salt and no added ghee can lower sodium and saturated fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~26 per serving) despite low GI (50) due to large carbohydrate portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Palak Khichdi has a low GI (~50) owing to the moong dal and spinach content, but the glycemic load per standard 300 g serving is moderate (~26); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 150–200 g and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and beneficial micronutrients (folate, iron, vitamin A from spinach)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Palak Khichdi is broadly cardioprotective due to its high fibre content (~1.8 g/100 g), low saturated fat (~1.5 g/100 g), and spinach-derived folate and antioxidants; ghee should be minimised or replaced with a small amount of olive oil for individuals with elevated LDL cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Palak Khichdi is a traditional Indian one-pot comfort dish made by cooking rice and moong dal (split yellow lentils) together with fresh spinach (palak) until soft and porridge-like, then finishing with a spiced ghee tempering (tadka) of cumin seeds, turmeric, ginger, garlic, and green chilli. The dish has a soft, creamy texture and a mildly earthy, savoury flavour with a subtle bitterness from the spinach balanced by the richness of ghee. It is widely consumed across North and South India as a wholesome meal for all ages, including infants and the elderly, and is a common choice during illness or recovery due to its digestibility. Nutritionally, Palak Khichdi provides approximately 112 kcal per 100 g, with 4.2 g protein, 17.5 g carbohydrate, 2.8 g fat, and 1.8 g dietary fibre. The spinach component contributes meaningful amounts of vitamin A (~95 mcg RAE/100 g), folate (~38 mcg DFE/100 g), iron (~1.4 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (~5.2 mg/100 g). The dish has a low glycemic index (~50) due to the lentil content, making it a relatively suitable carbohydrate source for glycaemic management when portioned appropriately. In Singapore, it is served in Indian restaurants and is available on food delivery platforms. It is suitable for vegetarians and is naturally gluten-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in palak khichdi?
Is palak khichdi good for diabetes?
What is palak khichdi made of?
Is khichdi with spinach good for weight loss?
Is palak khichdi suitable for hypertension?

ingredient_synonyms.moong dal: split yellow mung lentils, mung bean dal

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry for Palak Khichdi exists. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of ICMR-NIN 2017 component entries (boiled rice, cooked moong dal, cooked spinach, ghee). GI is an analogous estimate based on moong dal khichdi data from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are authoritative and the recipe proportions are well-established.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from ICMR-NIN 2017 component entries; no single validated entry for Palak Khichdi exists in any approved primary source. A direct laboratory analysis would upgrade confidence to high. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients other than ghee, which contains negligible B12; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN component data. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no hydrogenated fats used; ghee contains only trace naturally-occurring trans fats below detection threshold in ICMR-NIN data. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source database for any of the component ingredients at the level of precision required. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source database for the component ingredients. 6. GI set to 50 (low) based on analogous moong dal khichdi data; addition of spinach and ghee expected to maintain low GI. GL per 300 g serving = (50 × 17.5 × 3) / 100 = 26.3. 7. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input source data appears to be a platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded. 8. Dietary flag is_vegan is false because ghee (clarified butter) is a standard ingredient; is_dairy_free is false for the same reason.
